bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: Django URL Routing — urls.py Patterns, Parameters, and include()
bytes.body:
Django's URL dispatcher maps request paths to views via `urlpatterns` lists in `urls.py`. Each entry is a `path()` or `re_path()` call. `path()` uses angle-bracket converters (`<int:pk>`, `<slug:slug>`, `<uuid:id>`) that both match and cast the value before it reaches your view. `re_path()` accepts raw regex for edge cases.

Grouping and prefixing routes is done with `include()`. You define app-level `urlpatterns` in each app's `urls.py`, then mount them under a prefix in the project-level `urls.py`. Django also supports `app_name` namespacing — set it in the app's `urls.py` and reference routes as `'blog:post-detail'` in `reverse()` or `{% url %}`, preventing name collisions across apps. Converters are extensible: register a custom one via `register_converter()` if the five built-ins (`str`, `int`, `slug`, `uuid`, `path`) don't cover your pattern.
bytes.code_snippet:
# blog/urls.py
from django.urls import path
from . import views

app_name = 'blog'

urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.post_list, name='post-list'),
    path('<int:pk>/', views.post_detail, name='post-detail'),
    path('<slug:slug>/edit/', views.post_edit, name='post-edit'),
]

# project/urls.py
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path('blog/', include('blog.urls')),
]

# Reverse: reverse('blog:post-detail', kwargs={'pk': 42})

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: Django ORM — Killing N+1 Queries Before They Kill Your App
bytes.body: Django's ORM is a full ORM — it maps Python objects to SQL rows and manages relationships automatically. That power comes with a trap: the N+1 query problem. Accessing a related object inside a loop without prefetching fires one extra query per row. Fix it with `select_related` for ForeignKey/OneToOne (SQL JOIN) and `prefetch_related` for ManyToMany/reverse FK (separate IN query, Python-side join). Use `django-debug-toolbar` or log `django.db.backends` to spot N+1s in development. For complex filtering, Django's `Q` objects and `annotate()` with `F` expressions let you push logic into SQL rather than Python. Avoid raw `queryset.filter()` loops — compose querysets lazily and evaluate once. On the migration side, always review `makemigrations` output before applying; use `RunSQL` for custom indexes and `SeparateDatabaseAndState` when renaming models to avoid table rebuilds. Never run `migrate` without `--plan` in production.
bytes.code_snippet:
from django.db.models import Prefetch

# Bad: N+1 — one query per post's author
posts = Post.objects.all()
for post in posts:
    print(post.author.username)  # hits DB each iteration

# Good: 1 JOIN query
posts = Post.objects.select_related('author').all()

# Good: prefetch tags (M2M) with a filtered queryset
posts = Post.objects.prefetch_related(
    Prefetch('tags', queryset=Tag.objects.filter(active=True))
).select_related('author')

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: Django Project Structure — App-per-Feature vs Traditional MTV Layering
bytes.body:
Django's default layout encourages a flat `models.py / views.py / urls.py` per app, which works fine at small scale but degrades fast. Once an app crosses ~5 models, you end up with 800-line files and circular imports.

The vertical slice pattern reorganizes each Django app into a self-contained feature folder:

```
users/
  models.py
  services.py      # business logic, no HTTP
  selectors.py     # read queries (Django ORM)
  views.py         # thin — calls services/selectors only
  urls.py
  serializers.py
  tests/
```

This mirrors HackSoft's Django Styleguide and maps cleanly onto Django's app registry — each folder is still a proper `INSTALLED_APP`. The key rule: **views never touch ORM directly**. They call `selectors.get_user_by_id()` or `services.create_user()`, keeping Django's ORM power in a testable layer. Split `models.py` into `models/user.py` + `models/__init__.py` once it exceeds ~3 models. Use `django.urls.include()` to compose app-level `urls.py` into the root router cleanly.
bytes.code_snippet:
# users/selectors.py
from django.db.models import QuerySet
from .models import User

def get_active_users() -> QuerySet[User]:
    return User.objects.filter(is_active=True).select_related("profile")

# users/services.py
from .models import User

def deactivate_user(*, user_id: int) -> User:
    user = User.objects.get(pk=user_id)
    user.is_active = False
    user.save(update_fields=["is_active"])
    return user

# users/views.py
from django.http import JsonResponse
from . import selectors

def active_users_view(request):
    qs = selectors.get_active_users()
    return JsonResponse({"count": qs.count()})

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: Django Middleware — Request Pipeline, Ordering, and Route-Specific Patterns
bytes.body:
Django's middleware stack is a list of callables in `MIDDLEWARE` (settings.py) that wraps every request/response cycle. Order is critical: middleware processes requests top-down and responses bottom-up. `SecurityMiddleware` must sit first to enforce HTTPS redirects before anything else runs; `SessionMiddleware` must precede `AuthenticationMiddleware` since auth depends on the session.

Each middleware class implements `__init__(self, get_response)` and `__call__(self, request)`. Call `get_response(request)` to pass control down the chain. Hook into `process_view`, `process_exception`, or `process_template_response` for finer-grained interception.

Django has no native per-route middleware, but you can simulate it using a decorator-based approach with `django.utils.decorators.decorator_from_middleware`, or by conditionally short-circuiting inside `__call__` based on `request.path`. For async views, inherit from `django.utils.deprecation.MiddlewareMixin` or write a pure async middleware with `async def __call__`. Misordering — e.g., placing `GZipMiddleware` after `SecurityMiddleware` — silently breaks compression on HTTPS responses.
bytes.code_snippet:
# settings.py — order matters
MIDDLEWARE = [
    'django.middleware.security.SecurityMiddleware',   # must be first
    'django.contrib.sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware',
    'django.middleware.common.CommonMiddleware',
    'django.contrib.auth.middleware.AuthenticationMiddleware',  # needs session
    'myapp.middleware.RequestTimingMiddleware',
]

# myapp/middleware.py
import time

class RequestTimingMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, get_response):
        self.get_response = get_response

    def __call__(self, request):
        start = time.monotonic()
        response = self.get_response(request)
        duration = time.monotonic() - start
        response['X-Request-Duration'] = f'{duration:.4f}s'
        return response

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: Django Dependency Injection — Manual Wiring vs Module Imports and What It Costs You in Tests
bytes.body:
Django ships with no DI container. The default pattern is direct module imports — your view or service just calls `from myapp.services import send_email`, creating a hidden, hard-coded dependency. This works until you need to test: you're forced to `mock.patch('myapp.services.send_email')` with the full import path, and one refactor silently breaks the patch string.

The alternative is manual constructor injection in service classes. Define `EmailService` as a plain class, accept it as a parameter in your view or business layer, and wire it at the call site or via a factory. In tests, you swap in a fake with zero patching. Django's `override_settings` covers settings-bound dependencies (e.g., swapping `EMAIL_BACKEND`), but for custom services it's insufficient.

For larger projects, `django-injector` (built on `injector`) brings container-style binding without magic — you declare a `Module`, bind interfaces to implementations, and let the container resolve the graph. The payoff is explicit dependency graphs, easy test doubles, and no fragile patch strings.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: Django Input Validation — Schema Validation, Sanitization, and Error Responses at the Boundary
bytes.body: Django Forms and DRF Serializers are your validation boundary — never let raw request data reach your business logic. Use `serializers.Serializer` (or `ModelSerializer`) to declare schema upfront: field types, `required`, `allow_null`, `max_length`, and custom `validate_<field>` methods run automatically on `.is_valid()`. Call `.is_valid(raise_exception=True)` in DRF views to auto-return a 400 with structured error detail — no manual error shaping needed. For sanitization, DRF doesn't HTML-escape by default; if you're storing user content rendered in a browser, run it through `bleach.clean()` before saving. Avoid `request.data` directly in model constructors or raw SQL — always go through a validated serializer. For nested objects, use nested serializers with `many=True` or `allow_empty=False` rather than iterating raw lists. Custom cross-field validation belongs in `validate()` (the object-level method), not in the view. This keeps validation declarative, testable, and co-located with your schema.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: Django Authentication — JWT Validation, Sessions, and Middleware Patterns
bytes.body:
Django's default session backend stores session IDs in cookies backed by the database (or cache). When you introduce JWTs via `djangorestframework-simplejwt`, the two systems coexist but serve different clients — sessions for browser views, JWTs for API consumers.

`simplejwt` validates tokens in `JWTAuthentication.authenticate()`, which checks the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header, decodes the JWT using `SECRET_KEY` (or an RS256 key pair), and raises `InvalidToken` on failure. Set `AUTH_HEADER_TYPES`, `ACCESS_TOKEN_LIFETIME`, and `ROTATE_REFRESH_TOKENS` in `SIMPLE_JWT` settings.

For custom middleware — e.g. attaching a tenant or rate-limit scope from JWT claims — subclass `MiddlewareMixin` or write a pure ASGI callable. Read `request.auth` (populated by DRF after authentication runs) rather than re-decoding the token yourself. Never place JWT logic in Django's `MIDDLEWARE` list before DRF's authenticators fire; middleware runs before view dispatch, so `request.user` is still `AnonymousUser` at that point. Use DRF `permission_classes` and `authentication_classes` per-view or globally in `DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES`.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: Django Global Error Handling — Consistent JSON Error Responses
bytes.body:
Django's default error responses return HTML, which breaks API clients expecting JSON. Fix this by subclassing `JsonResponse` inside a custom exception handler registered via Django REST Framework's `EXCEPTION_HANDLER` setting — or, for plain Django, by overriding `handler400`, `handler403`, `handler404`, and `handler500` in your root `urls.py`.

For DRF projects, define a custom handler in a utils module and point `REST_FRAMEWORK['EXCEPTION_HANDLER']` at it. Inside, call `exception_handler(exc, context)` first to get DRF's default response, then reformat it into a stable envelope: `{"error": {"code": ..., "message": ..., "detail": ...}}`. This guarantees every 4xx/5xx the API emits shares the same shape.

For unhandled exceptions (500s), DRF's handler returns `None` — explicitly catch that case and return a sanitized `JsonResponse` with `status=500` so stack traces never leak to clients. Pair this with Django's `LOGGING` config to route those exceptions to your error tracker (e.g., Sentry via `sentry_sdk.init`) before responding.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: Django Testing — Unit vs Integration, Mocking HTTP, and Test Database Strategy
bytes.body:
Django's `TestCase` wraps each test in a transaction that rolls back after the test — giving you a clean DB without teardown code. Use `SimpleTestCase` for logic with no DB access (forms, validators, template tags) and `TransactionTestCase` only when you need to test actual commit behavior or signals that fire post-commit.

For unit tests, isolate at the view or service layer using `unittest.mock.patch`. When testing code that calls external APIs (Stripe, Twilio), patch the requests call or use `responses` library to register fake endpoints — never hit live services in CI.

For integration tests, Django's `Client` and `RequestFactory` are your tools. `Client` runs the full middleware stack; `RequestFactory` skips it. Prefer `Client` for testing auth, sessions, and middleware behavior. Use `@override_settings(DATABASES=...)` to point tests at a faster in-memory SQLite DB during unit runs, while keeping PostgreSQL for integration suites. Fixture loading via `fixtures = ['seed.json']` is fragile — prefer `baker.make()` (model-bakery) or factory-boy for maintainable test data.

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: Django Security Hardening — CORS, Rate Limiting, Headers, and Injection Prevention
bytes.body:
Django ships with solid defaults, but production security requires explicit configuration. For CORS, use `django-cors-headers`: set `CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS` to an explicit list — never `CORS_ALLOW_ALL_ORIGINS = True` in production. Pair it with `CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS = False` unless your auth flow demands otherwise.

For rate limiting, `django-ratelimit` gives you decorator-level control. Use `@ratelimit(key='ip', rate='20/m', block=True)` on login and registration views to blunt brute-force attacks.

Security headers are handled by `django-csp` for Content-Security-Policy and Django's built-ins: set `SECURE_BROWSER_XSS_FILTER`, `X_FRAME_OPTIONS = 'DENY'`, `SECURE_CONTENT_TYPE_NOSNIFF = True`, and `SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS = 31536000` in settings.

For injection prevention, never interpolate user input into raw SQL — use the ORM or `cursor.execute()` with parameterized queries. Django's ORM escapes values automatically, but `RawQuerySet` and `extra()` are foot-guns. Validate all external input with serializers or `django.core.validators` before it touches the database layer.
bytes.code_snippet:
# settings.py — production security baseline
CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS = ["https://app.example.com"]
CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS = False

SECURE_BROWSER_XSS_FILTER = True
SECURE_CONTENT_TYPE_NOSNIFF = True
X_FRAME_OPTIONS = "DENY"
SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS = 31536000
SECURE_HSTS_INCLUDE_SUBDOMAINS = True

# views.py — rate-limit login endpoint
from django_ratelimit.decorators import ratelimit

@ratelimit(key="ip", rate="10/m", block=True)
def login_view(request):
    ...

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: Django Performance Trio: Connection Pooling, Async Views, and GZip Middleware
bytes.body:
Django's default database layer opens a new connection per request unless you configure pooling. Use `django-db-geventpool` or PgBouncer externally, or set `CONN_MAX_AGE` in `DATABASES` to reuse connections — `CONN_MAX_AGE=60` keeps each thread's connection alive for 60 seconds, eliminating per-request TCP handshakes.

For I/O-bound views, Django's native async support (3.1+) lets you define `async def` views and use `sync_to_async` to wrap ORM calls. Pair this with an ASGI server like Uvicorn or Daphne — running under WSGI silently downgrades async views to sync execution.

Response compression is handled by `django.middleware.gzip.GZipMiddleware`. Add it near the top of `MIDDLEWARE` (above `SessionMiddleware`) so it wraps the full response. For streaming responses, use `StreamingHttpResponse` with `GZipMiddleware` — it chunks the output, keeping memory overhead flat.

Combined: persistent DB connections reduce latency, async views increase throughput under I/O load, and GZip cuts payload size — three levers that compose cleanly without external dependencies.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: Django REST Framework — Serialization, camelCase, and Null Field Handling
bytes.body:
DRF serializers are your request/response DTOs. Define them explicitly rather than relying on ModelSerializer auto-fields — it gives you full control over shape and validation. For camelCase output (required by most JS frontends), use `djangorestframework-camel-case`: wrap your renderer and parser in settings and field names automatically convert between snake_case internally and camelCase externally — zero manual mapping.

For null handling, DRF's `allow_null=True` permits null input, but pair it with `required=False` if the field is optional on writes. On reads, set `default=None` only when the field can be legitimately absent from the DB row. Avoid `SerializerMethodField` for simple transformations — use a custom field or override `to_representation()` on the serializer instead. For write-only fields (passwords, tokens), set `write_only=True`; for computed read-only fields, use `read_only=True`. Validate cross-field logic in `validate()`, not `validate_<field>()`, to access the full `data` dict.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: Django REST Framework — Versioning, URL Structure, and Auto OpenAPI Docs
bytes.body:
Django REST Framework ships with four versioning schemes. `URLPathVersioning` is the most explicit — it embeds the version in the URL (`/api/v1/users/`) and makes routing unambiguous. Configure it globally in `REST_FRAMEWORK` settings via `DEFAULT_VERSIONING_CLASS` and `ALLOWED_VERSIONS`, then access `request.version` inside any `APIView` or `ViewSet` to branch behavior without forking serializers unnecessarily.

For OpenAPI generation, DRF 3.14+ includes `drf-spectacular` as the community standard. Add `SpectacularAPIView` and `SpectacularSwaggerView` to `urlpatterns` for interactive docs. Annotate non-obvious endpoints with `@extend_schema(parameters=[...], responses={200: MySerializer})` — this keeps the schema accurate without writing YAML by hand.

Avoid versioning via query params (`?version=1`) — it breaks HTTP caching and makes URL semantics unclear. Accept headers (`AcceptHeaderVersioning`) are REST-pure but harder to test in browsers. Stick with URL path versioning for public APIs, namespace your routers per version, and deprecate old versions by returning a `Deprecation` response header alongside a sunset date.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: Django Settings — Env Vars, Split Configs, and Secrets Without Leaks
bytes.body: Django's single `settings.py` breaks down fast in real projects. The idiomatic fix: split into `settings/base.py`, `settings/local.py`, and `settings/production.py`, then point `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE` at the right one per environment. For secrets, use `django-environ` or `python-decouple` to pull values from `.env` files or OS environment variables — never hardcode `SECRET_KEY` or database credentials. `django-environ` lets you cast types inline: `env('DEBUG', cast=bool, default=False)` and parse `DATABASE_URL` into Django's `DATABASES` dict with a single `env.db()` call. For production secrets on Azure or AWS, mount them as environment variables via your container runtime or use `django-storages` with Azure Key Vault. Always set `SECRET_KEY` via env, validate it's not the default in `AppConfig.ready()`, and add `.env` to `.gitignore`. Use `django-environ`'s `EnvError` to fail fast on missing required vars at startup rather than at runtime. Keep `ALLOWED_HOSTS`, `CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS`, and `DATABASES` out of version control entirely.
bytes.code_snippet:
# settings/base.py
import environ

env = environ.Env(
    DEBUG=(bool, False),
)
environ.Env.read_env()  # reads .env in project root

SECRET_KEY = env('SECRET_KEY')  # raises EnvError if missing
DEBUG = env('DEBUG')
DATABASES = {'default': env.db('DATABASE_URL')}
ALLOWED_HOSTS = env.list('ALLOWED_HOSTS', default=[])

# settings/production.py
from .base import *

SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT = True
SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: Django Structured Logging — Correlation IDs and Request Middleware
bytes.body:
Django's default logging uses Python's `logging` module, but plain-text logs break in distributed systems. Switch to structured JSON logs using `python-json-logger` and inject a correlation ID per request so you can trace a full lifecycle across services.

Create a middleware that reads `X-Request-ID` from headers (or generates a UUID fallback), stores it in `threading.local()`, and makes it available to a custom `logging.Filter`. That filter injects the correlation ID into every log record emitted during that request.

Configure `LOGGING` in `settings.py` to use `pythonjsonlogger.jsonlogger.JsonFormatter`. Every log call — including Django's built-in `django.request` logger — will emit structured JSON with `correlation_id`, `level`, `timestamp`, and your custom fields. Pair this with `structlog` if you need context accumulation (e.g., attaching `user_id` mid-request). In production, ship these JSON lines directly to CloudWatch, Datadog, or Loki without any parsing regex.
bytes.code_snippet:
# middleware.py
import uuid, logging, threading
from django.utils.deprecation import MiddlewareMixin

_local = threading.local()

class CorrelationIdMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin):
    def process_request(self, request):
        _local.correlation_id = request.headers.get("X-Request-ID", str(uuid.uuid4()))
        request.correlation_id = _local.correlation_id

class CorrelationIdFilter(logging.Filter):
    def filter(self, record):
        record.correlation_id = getattr(_local, "correlation_id", "-")
        return True

# settings.py snippet
# Add filter to your handler and set formatter to JsonFormatter

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: Django Background Jobs — Celery, Beat, and Worker Process Patterns
bytes.body:
Django has no built-in task queue, but Celery is the de facto standard. Define tasks with @shared_task (preferred over @app.task) so they're decoupled from the Celery app instance — critical for reusable Django apps. Tasks are dispatched via .delay() or .apply_async(), the latter giving control over eta, countdown, queue routing, and retry policy via autoretry_for and max_retries. Bind the task (bind=True) to access self.retry() for manual retry logic with exponential backoff.

For scheduled jobs, celery-beat reads a CELERY_BEAT_SCHEDULE dict in settings.py and enqueues tasks on a cron or interval. In production, store the schedule in the database using django-celery-beat so you can edit it at runtime without a redeploy.

Run workers with celery -A myproject worker -Q default,priority --concurrency=4. Use separate named queues (-Q) to isolate slow tasks (e.g. report generation) from fast ones (e.g. sending emails). Always set CELERY_TASK_ALWAYS_EAGER = True in tests to execute tasks synchronously without a broker.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: Django Monitoring — Health Checks, Prometheus Metrics, and Distributed Tracing
bytes.body: Django doesn't ship with health check or metrics endpoints out of the box, but the ecosystem fills the gap cleanly. `django-health-check` lets you register checks (database, cache, storage, custom) under a single `/health/` view by adding `health_check` and its sub-apps to `INSTALLED_APPS` and wiring `HealthCheckView` in `urls.py`. For Prometheus metrics, `django-prometheus` instruments your models, views, migrations, and DB queries automatically — just prepend `django_prometheus.middleware.PrometheusBeforeMiddleware` and append `PrometheusAfterMiddleware`, then expose `/metrics/`. For distributed tracing, `opentelemetry-instrumentation-django` hooks into Django's request/response cycle via `DjangoInstrumentor().instrument()`, automatically propagating W3C trace context across services. Pair it with `opentelemetry-instrumentation-psycopg2` to get DB span visibility. All three integrate with Gunicorn and `ASGI` deployments — call `DjangoInstrumentor` in `AppConfig.ready()` rather than `settings.py` to avoid double-instrumentation in multi-worker setups. Use `HEALTH_CHECK` dict in settings to configure thresholds like disk usage warnings.
bytes.code_snippet:
# apps.py
from django.apps import AppConfig
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.django import DjangoInstrumentor
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.psycopg2 import Psycopg2Instrumentor

class CoreConfig(AppConfig):
    name = 'core'

    def ready(self):
        DjangoInstrumentor().instrument()
        Psycopg2Instrumentor().instrument()

# urls.py
from health_check.views import MainView
from django_prometheus import exports

urlpatterns = [
    path('health/', MainView.as_view()),
    path('metrics/', exports.ExportToDjangoView),
]

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: Django's Dev Tooling Stack — Hot Reload, Management Commands, and django-extensions
bytes.body:
Django's built-in `runserver` auto-reloads on file save via `StatReloader` (default) or `WatchmanReloader` if pywatchman is installed — Watchman is significantly faster on large codebases. Enable it by installing `pywatchman` and ensuring `DJANGO_WATCHMAN_TIMEOUT` is set if needed.

For code generation and scaffolding, `startapp` is just the beginning. `django-extensions` adds `show_urls`, `shell_plus` (auto-imports all models), and critically `generate_secret_key`. For full scaffold generation, `djangorestframework` pairs with `django-rest-framework-stubs` for typed ViewSet generation.

The real power is custom management commands. Drop a file in `yourapp/management/commands/seed_db.py`, subclass `BaseCommand`, and wire it to `handle()` — you get argument parsing, `--verbosity`, and `--no-color` for free. Combine with `call_command()` in tests for isolated fixture seeding. For async-aware dev workflows, `DJANGO_ALLOW_ASYNC_UNSAFE=true` unblocks ORM calls in async shells without full ASGI setup.
bytes.code_snippet:
# yourapp/management/commands/seed_db.py
from django.core.management.base import BaseCommand
from yourapp.models import Article

class Command(BaseCommand):
    help = 'Seed articles for local dev'

    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('--count', type=int, default=10)

    def handle(self, *args, **options):
        count = options['count']
        articles = [Article(title=f'Article {i}') for i in range(count)]
        Article.objects.bulk_create(articles)
        self.stdout.write(self.style.SUCCESS(f'Seeded {count} articles'))

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: Django's Batteries-Included Admin: Customizing Beyond the Defaults
bytes.body:
Django's admin isn't just a CRUD scaffold — it's a fully extensible framework. Most developers stop at `list_display` and `search_fields`, but the real power lies in `ModelAdmin` hooks and inline formsets.

`list_select_related` prevents N+1 queries when displaying related fields. `get_queryset()` lets you scope data per-user without touching views. `save_model()` lets you inject business logic at save time — perfect for audit trails or triggering signals without duplicating service logic.

Inlines (`TabularInline`, `StackedInline`) let you edit related models on the same page. Combine with `extra = 0` and `can_delete = False` for tight UX control.

`readonly_fields` can point to callables, letting you render computed properties or formatted HTML directly in the change view — no template override needed.

For bulk operations, `actions` accepts callables with `(modeladmin, request, queryset)` signatures, giving you queryset-level control with full ORM access. Add `@admin.action(description='...')` for clean labeling.

The admin is production-grade tooling if you treat it like code, not config.
